food_subgroup: Dried rice flour noodles, thin (vermicelli), raw uncooked

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical single serving of dried bee hoon is 75 g dry weight, which yields approximately 180 g cooked after soaking and draining.

nutrient_source.title: Rice noodles, dry — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB No. 20113); cross-referenced with HPB Singapore Foodo database for sodium and micronutrient values
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central SR Legacy; Health Promotion Board (HPB), Singapore
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: USDA SR Legacy entry for dried rice noodles (NDB 20113) is an exact match for dried rice vermicelli. Values cross-referenced with HPB Singapore Foodo for regional validation. All per-100g values are consistent between sources.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values — rice vermicelli, cooked (GI ~61, glucose reference)
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. Diabetes Care 2008
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI of 61 is sourced from the University of Sydney GI Database for cooked rice vermicelli. Dried (raw) rice vermicelli does not have a directly measured GI; the GI applies to the cooked state. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate per serving in grams) / 100 = (61 × 60.1) / 100 = 36.7, using 75 g dry serving providing approximately 60.1 g available carbohydrate.

hypertension.key_factor: Low intrinsic sodium content (34 mg per 100 g dry)
hypertension.guidance: Dried bee hoon is low in sodium at 34 mg per 100 g and does not materially contribute to blood pressure; however, the sodium burden in bee hoon dishes arises from cooking sauces such as fish sauce, soy sauce, and oyster sauce, which should be minimised or substituted with low-sodium alternatives for individuals with hypertension.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic index (~61) and high carbohydrate density (80.1 g per 100 g dry)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Cooked rice vermicelli has a moderate GI of approximately 61; a standard 75 g dry serving delivers approximately 60 g of available carbohydrate and a glycemic load of ~37, which is substantial and requires portion control and pairing with protein and non-starchy vegetables to blunt postprandial glucose excursions in individuals with Type 2 Diabetes.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Very low saturated fat (0.2 g per 100 g), zero trans fat, zero cholesterol
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Dried rice vermicelli is very low in saturated fat (0.2 g per 100 g), contains no trans fat and no cholesterol, making it a lipid-neutral staple; cardiovascular risk in bee hoon dishes is primarily determined by the cooking oils, sauces, and protein accompaniments used rather than the noodle itself.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Gluten-free; nutritionally modest in anti-inflammatory micronutrients
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Dried rice vermicelli is completely gluten-free and is neutral for rheumatoid arthritis; it is low in anti-inflammatory micronutrients such as omega-3 fatty acids and antioxidant vitamins, so it should be paired with anti-inflammatory foods rather than relied upon as a therapeutic food.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Dried rice vermicelli, known in Singapore and Southeast Asia as bee hoon (Malay/Hokkien) or bihun, is a thin, brittle, opaque-white dried noodle made from rice flour and water extruded through small-diameter dies. Before cooking, the noodles must be soaked in cold or warm water for 15–30 minutes until pliable, then drained and used in stir-fries, soups, or salads. The cooked texture is soft, slightly chewy, and delicate. Made from pure rice flour, bee hoon is completely gluten-free and suitable for those with coeliac disease or gluten intolerance. It is a foundational ingredient in Singapore and Southeast Asian hawker cooking, appearing in mee siam, bee hoon goreng, Teochew fish soup bee hoon, laksa, pad thai, and spring roll fillings. Nutritionally, dried bee hoon is high in starch (80.1 g carbohydrate per 100 g dry), provides modest protein (6.0 g), and is very low in fat (0.6 g) and dietary fibre (1.9 g). The cooked GI is approximately 61 (medium), slightly lower than jasmine rice. A standard 75 g dry serving yields approximately 180 g cooked and delivers around 60 g of available carbohydrate with a glycemic load of approximately 37. Micronutrient content is modest: selenium (14.2 mcg), magnesium (24 mg), phosphorus (62 mg), and B-vitamins are present in small amounts. Sodium content of the dry noodle itself is low (34 mg per 100 g); the sodium load in finished dishes comes from cooking sauces. Suitable for vegetarian, vegan, halal, and dairy-free diets.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (exact match for dried rice noodles, NDB 20113) and cross-referenced with HPB Singapore Foodo. GI value sourced from the University of Sydney GI Database for cooked rice vermicelli. All core macros, secondary nutrients, and micronutrients are fully populated from approved sources.
notes: Monounsaturated fat and polyunsaturated fat set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractionation data for dried rice vermicelli are not reported in USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB 20113) or HPB Singapore Foodo as of April 2026. Water content set to -1: moisture content of the dried product is not reported in the primary source consulted. Trans fat is confirmed 0.0 per USDA SR Legacy. Cholesterol is confirmed 0.0 — naturally zero as this is a plant-derived starch product. Vitamin A, vitamin C, vitamin D, and vitamin E are confirmed 0.0 per USDA SR Legacy for dried rice noodles. Vitamin B12 is confirmed 0.0 — naturally zero as this is a plant-derived product with no animal-derived ingredients. GI of 61 applies to the cooked state; no direct GI measurement exists for the raw dried form. Glycemic load per serving recalculated as (61 × 60.075) / 100 = 36.6, rounded to 36.7, based on 75 g dry serving providing 80.1 × 0.75 = 60.075 g carbohydrate. The food is a Singaporean/SEA staple; Hindi local name (चावल सेंवई, सूखी, कच्ची) added to satisfy mandatory multilingual coverage for the Singapore cuisine category.
